title: Sending data to an unbound port leads exposed container ports to close
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->
During the creation of an OCI container from an image via podman it is possible to specify port bindings between host and container, even if the image from which that container is built does not expose these ports. When using curl or nc to send requests on these ports ALL specified container ports close without a warning and the state of the container is still "running".
This behavior is rather unexpected and it took me long to figure out this bug. Docker does not close all other ports and this is what I would expect from podman as well. 

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create a container from the latest nginx image using rootless podman:
`podman run -d --name webserver -p 8080:80 -p 8443:443 nginx:latest`
Notice, that the nginx image has only port 80 exposed by default, which can be verified via
`podman inspect nginx:latest`

2. Verify that nginx is up and running and has its exposed port 80 bound to the host port 8080:
`curl 127.0.0.1:8080
-> HTML from nginx example website showing up
`
3. Send a curl request to port 8443, which is bound to an non-exposed image port of the container, i.e., port 443:
`curl 127.0.0.1:8443
curl: (56) Recv failure: Connection reset by peer
`
The container is still in running state, which can be verified via
`podman inspect webserver`

**Describe the results you received:**
When now checking again the port 8080, which worked before the request to the non-exposed port, it fails.
`curl 127.0.0.1:8080
curl: (7) Failed to connect to 127.0.0.1 port 8084: Connection refused
`

**Describe the results you expected:**
I expect the port 8084 not to be closed but still working. It would also be beneficial to get a warning message either when the container is created or when trying to communicate with the port. Also possible, even if not elegant would be to put the running container in an error state, indicating that something went wrong. 

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
Docker does not show the above described problem. The port 8084 stays open and functioning even after the request to the non-exposed image port.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:            1.8.2
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.10.1
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
debug:                                          
  compiler: gc 
  git commit: ""               
  go version: go1.10.1
  podman version: 1.8.2                           
host:

  Conmon:  
    package: 'conmon: /usr/libexec/podman/conmon'
    path: /usr/libexec/podman/conmon                                                                               
    version: 'conmon version 2.0.14, commit: '
  Distribution:
    distribution: ubuntu
    version: "18.04"
  IDMappings:                                                                                                      
    gidmap:

      host_id: 996

      host_id: 165536
      size: 65536                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
    uidmap:
    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 998
      size: 1
    - container_id: 1
      host_id: 165536
      size: 65536

  MemTotal: 25264590848
  OCIRuntime:
    name: runc
    package: 'containerd.io: /usr/bin/runc'

    version: |-
      runc version 1.0.0-rc10
      commit: dc9208a3303feef5b3839f4323d9beb36df0a9dd
      spec: 1.0.1-dev
  SwapFree: 31999389696
  SwapTotal: 31999389696
  arch: amd64
  cpus: 16
  eventlogger: journald
  hostname: fauamip2f
  kernel: 4.15.0-96-generic
  os: linux
  rootless: true
  slirp4netns:
    Executable: /usr/bin/slirp4netns
    Package: 'slirp4netns: /usr/bin/slirp4netns'
    Version: |-
      slirp4netns version 0.4.3
      commit: unknown
  uptime: 70h 33m 16.27s (Approximately 2.92 days)
registries:
  search:
  - docker.io
  - quay.io
store:
  ConfigFile: /home.local/podman/.config/containers/storage.conf
  ContainerStore:
    number: 4
  GraphDriverName: vfs
  GraphOptions: {}
  GraphRoot: /home.local/podman/.local/share/containers/storage
  GraphStatus: {}
  ImageStore:
    number: 4
  RunRoot: /run/user/998
  VolumePath: /home.local/podman/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes

```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
podman/unknown,now 1.8.2~144 amd64 [installed]
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
Are you running as root, or rootless?

There is no distinction anywhere within Podman as to whether a port was exposed by the image, or by the user; all port mappings are grouped together at time of container creation. I'm wondering if this isn't caused by the forwarding process crashing after initial traffic is received.

Thanks for your explanation. I am running rootless.
This happens with any port I try except port 80, which is declared as "exposed" by the nginx image.
Can you confirm that `nginx` in the container is actually listening on port 443? Could be this is caused by hitting a port with nothing listening in the container.
No, `nginx` is only listening on port 80. The configuration file `nginx.conf` and its includes have no line `listen 443;`.

I may have run into this, as well. I decided to trial `podman` at hosting an in-house security ctf event, and had it running 41 pods for about 7 hours before I noticed some service ports that were previously checked and verified working had suddenly closed. The container logs show no error, the app is still running inside. But the host-bound port, when connected to via `nc` to `127.0.0.1` from the `podman` host, was now refusing connections. Restarting the container did not fix. Deleting and restarting the pod using `podman play kube` did not fix! The host-bound port was permanently cursed. Changing the host-bound port defined in the `.yaml` fixed the issue, except we were unable to use the original port anymore. This was before the event started (pre-checks) so there were no attacks involved. Maybe one of the testers ran `nmap`, that wouldn't be a surprise.
If you still have the setup available, an `iptables -nvL` and `iptables -t nat -nvL` from the host would be greatly appreciate (assuming this was run as root).

It's still up. In my case it was a mix of root and rootless, mostly rootless. The container where the port broke was rootless. Missing port was `9003`. I do see it in `netstat`.

Given the container was rootless, probably a `slirp4netns` bug
